description: The provided image is unreadable (displaying only a generic 'Check for updates' icon), but based on the comprehensive text extraction summary and paper context regarding Fe–Mn–Al–C austenitic low-density steels, this Figure likely presents engineering stress-strain curves comparing mechanical properties across different alloy compositions and heat treatment conditions. The curves distinguish between Ti/V-free steel (M1) and Ti/V-modified steel (M2) in both solution-treated (SS) and aged states (550°C for 30 h). Key quantitative data visible in the text summary include: M1-SS exhibits a yield strength (YS) of approximately 430 MPa; M1-30 shows YS of 860 MPa, ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 1004 MPa, and elongation of 40%, but with significantly reduced work-hardening rate attributed to glide plane softening from sheared κ-carbides. In contrast, M2-SS displays YS of ~660 MPa, while M2-30 achieves YS of 884 MPa and UTS of 1096 MPa while maintaining work-hardening capacity comparable to the solution-treated state. The Figure supports the core conclusion that employing (Ti,V)C carbides as carbon sinks enables heterogeneous κ-carbide precipitation, thereby optimizing the strength-work-hardening synergy.
